mnemonics.title: POLY = MANY
mnemonics.content: POLYalphabetic = MANY alphabets. Unlike MONO (one), POLY uses multiple substitution alphabets controlled by a keyword, so the same letter encrypts differently at different positions.

mnemonics.title: KEY Repeats
mnemonics.content: Vigenere KEY repeats: K-E-Y-K-E-Y-K-E-Y... Each keyword letter tells you which Caesar shift to use. K=10, E=4, Y=24. The keyword cycles through the entire plaintext.

mnemonics.title: KASISKI Kills Vigenere
mnemonics.content: Kasiski's method: Find Repeated Sequences, Measure Distances, Compute GCD = Keyword Length. Then split into groups and frequency-analyze each group like a simple Caesar cipher.

mnemonics.title: IC = Identity Check
mnemonics.content: Index of Coincidence: English ~0.065, Random ~0.038. When you split ciphertext by the correct keyword length, each group's IC jumps to ~0.065 (natural language), confirming the right length.

mnemonics.title: Vigenere to OTP Pipeline
mnemonics.content: Short keyword = repeating = breakable Vigenere. Key as long as message + truly random + used once = unbreakable One-Time Pad. OTP is the perfect Vigenere.

keyPoints: Polyalphabetic ciphers use multiple substitution alphabets, controlled by a keyword, to encrypt a message.
The Vigenere cipher is the most well-known polyalphabetic cipher, using a repeating keyword to determine Caesar shifts.
It resists simple frequency analysis because the same plaintext letter maps to different ciphertext letters at different positions.
The Kasiski Examination and Index of Coincidence can determine the keyword length, after which frequency analysis breaks each sub-cipher.
When the keyword equals the message length and is truly random, the Vigenere cipher becomes a One-Time Pad.
commonMistakes: Confusing the Vigenere cipher (polyalphabetic, multiple shifts) with a monoalphabetic cipher (single fixed substitution).
Assuming the Vigenere cipher is unbreakable because it uses multiple alphabets; the repeating keyword creates exploitable patterns.
Forgetting that the keyword repeats cyclically, which is the fundamental weakness exploited by the Kasiski Examination.
Mixing up the Kasiski method (finds keyword LENGTH) with frequency analysis (finds the actual KEY letters after length is known).
